verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "If you wish that we should be your friends, why do you drag us to you against our will?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you wish that we should be your foes, why do you kill your foes?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: We neither drag you to us against your will, nor do we kill our foes; but whatever we do in our dealings with you, though we may do it contrary to your inclination, yet we do it from our love to you, that you may voluntarily correct yourselves, and live an amended life.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For no one lives against his will; and yet a boy, in order to learn this lesson of his own free will, is beaten contrary to his inclination, and that often by the very man that is most dear to him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And this, indeed, is what the kings would desire to say to you if they were to strike you, for to this end their power has been ordained of God.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you cry out even when they are not striking you.
